skill.name: cc-skill-backend-patterns
skill.description: Backend architecture patterns, API design, database optimization, and server-side best practices for Node.js, Express, and Next.js API routes.
skill.summary: Backend architecture patterns, API design, database optimization, and server-side best practices for...
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: Educational backend development patterns skill. Static analyzer flagged numerous false positives due to misidentification of code comments as shell commands, environment variable access as sensitive data exposure, and URL paths as reconnaissance. True positive finding: JWT secret accessed via process.env - standard secure practice. No malicious behavior detected.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access
low_findings.description: Code accesses process.env.JWT_SECRET for JWT token verification. This is a standard secure practice for configuration management and does not expose secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Shell Command Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer misidentified markdown documentation comments (# List resources, # Get single resource) as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are documentation comments, not code execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Python HTTP Library Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer misidentified JavaScript Date.now() and array operations as Python HTTP libraries at lines 433, 436, 444.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer misidentified REST API URL paths (/api/markets) as system reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer misidentified skill name in YAML frontmatter and section headings as weak crypto references.

content.value_statement: Backend applications often suffer from poor architecture, scalability issues, and security vulnerabilities. This skill provides proven patterns for API design, database optimization, caching, authentication, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Design RESTful API structures with proper resource naming and HTTP methods
Implement repository and service layer patterns for clean code architecture
Optimize database queries to prevent N+1 problems and improve performance
Implement caching strategies using Redis and cache-aside patterns
Build secure authentication systems with JWT and role-based access control
Handle errors gracefully with centralized error handlers and retry logic
limitations: Skill focuses on Node.js, Express, and Next.js API routes only
Does not cover frontend or client-side development
Database examples are specific to PostgreSQL/Supabase
Caching examples assume Redis as the cache store

use_cases.title: Design New API Endpoints
use_cases.description: Architects and developers building new REST APIs can apply the resource-based URL patterns and middleware approaches for consistent, maintainable API design.

use_cases.title: Optimize Slow Database Queries
use_cases.description: Developers experiencing performance issues can identify and fix N+1 query problems using batch fetching and query optimization patterns.

use_cases.title: Implement Secure Authentication
use_cases.description: Developers adding authentication to their applications can implement JWT validation and role-based access control following security best practices.

prompt_templates.title: Design API Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RESTful API structure for a [resource] resource. Include endpoints for listing, getting by ID, creating, updating, and deleting. Show query parameter examples for filtering and pagination.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Database Query
prompt_templates.description: I have a function that fetches a list of orders and then loops through each to get customer details. This is causing slow performance.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a function that fetches a list of orders and then loops through each to get customer details. This is causing slow performance. Show me how to refactor this using the batch fetch pattern to avoid N+1 queries.

prompt_templates.title: Add JWT Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement JWT token validation and a requireAuth middleware for protecting API routes. Include error handling for invalid tokens.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Caching Layer
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cache-aside pattern implementation using Redis for a getUser function. Include cache hit/miss logic and cache invalidation.

output_examples.input: Show me how to design a RESTful API for a products resource
output_examples.output:
GET /api/products - List all products
GET /api/products/:id - Get single product
POST /api/products - Create product
PUT /api/products/:id - Replace product
PATCH /api/products/:id - Update product
DELETE /api/products/:id - Delete product

Query params: ?status=active&sort=price&limit=20&offset=0

output_examples.input: How do I prevent N+1 queries when fetching orders with customers?

best_practices: Separate concerns using repository, service, and controller layers for maintainable code
Always validate input using schema validation libraries like Zod before processing
Implement caching strategically - cache expensive operations but invalidate properly
anti_patterns: Do not select all columns with SELECT * - specify only needed fields for better performance
Avoid nesting database calls in loops - use batch fetches instead
Do not expose raw database errors to clients - use centralized error handling

faq.question: What languages and frameworks does this skill cover?
faq.answer: This skill covers TypeScript patterns for Node.js, Express, and Next.js API routes. The concepts apply to other frameworks but code examples are specific to these technologies.

faq.question: How do I choose between PUT and PATCH?
faq.answer: Use PUT for complete resource replacement - the client sends all fields. Use PATCH for partial updates - the client sends only the fields to change.

faq.question: When should I use Redis vs in-memory caching?
faq.answer: Use Redis for distributed caching across multiple servers. Use in-memory caching for single-server applications or when sharing cache is not required.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication in serverless functions?
faq.answer: Validate JWT tokens at the function entry point using middleware. Extract user information from the token and attach to the request context.

faq.question: What is the recommended way to structure a backend project?
faq.answer: Organize by feature or layer. Common approach: routes/controllers handle HTTP, services contain business logic, repositories handle data access. Keep related code together.

faq.question: How do I implement rate limiting properly?
faq.answer: Use a distributed rate limiter like Redis for production. Track requests by IP or user ID. Set appropriate limits based on your API requirements and monitor for abuse.
